title: Fully Dynamic Euclidean Bi-Chromatic Matching in Sublinear Update Time

reviews.summary: This paper introduces the first fully dynamic algorithm for Euclidean bi-chromatic matching in R^2 that achieves sublinear worst-case update time. The approach uses a bottom-up, p-ary quadtree (restricted p-tree) combined with “implicit” matchings solved on aggregated sub-instances (transportation between subcell centers) and on-demand conversion to explicit matchings. For any ε in (0,1], the data structure maintains an expected O(1/ε)-approximate matching with O(n^ε · ε^{-1}) update time, and experiments show dramatic speedups over static recomputation while providing accurate estimates of the 1-Wasserstein distance on both synthetic and real datasets.
strengths: Introduces the first fully dynamic sublinear-update algorithm for Euclidean bi-chromatic matching, a core primitive for 1-Wasserstein distance estimation.
Proposes a bottom-up quadtree variant with implicit geometric transportation at cell centers that neatly decouples per-cell work from the total number of points.

The notion of maintaining implicit matches and converting to explicit ones when needed is elegant and practically relevant; the paper also sketches an explicit-maintenance variant via short augmenting paths.
Provides an open-source C++ implementation and evaluates on large-scale synthetic and real data (NYC taxi), demonstrating millisecond-level updates at scale (up to 10^6 samples).
Empirical accuracy is close to exact and significantly better than the worst-case bound, and dynamic maintenance decisively outperforms periodic static recomputation.
The high-level algorithmic ideas (p-tree organization, implicit/explicit matching, bottom-up maintenance) are clearly communicated, with intuitive figures and a structured pipeline.
The static algorithm is reworked bottom-up for clarity of subsequent dynamization.
Dynamic OT/EMD is a compelling and timely problem for ML applications such as drift monitoring; a sublinear-update solution can materially change what is feasible in practice.
The work bridges geometric approximation techniques and dynamic data-structural maintenance, with potential to inspire further dynamic geometric optimization results.
weaknesses: There is a critical tension between the claimed O(n^ε) update time and the stated per-cell subroutine bounds: IMPLICIT-MATCHING runs in O(p^5 log p log n), and leaf recomputation via Hungarian is O(|Y_v|^3) with |Y_v| ≤ p^2. With p^2 chosen as Θ(n^ε) (as suggested in the overview), these yield per-node work as large as n^{2.5ε}–n^{3ε}, seemingly incompatible with O(n^ε) updates unless parameters are calibrated differently or faster subroutines are used; the paper does not reconcile this.
The approximation guarantee is only in expectation (via a single random shift) and the adversary model is not specified; expected guarantees can be fragile under adaptive updates.

The dynamic model oscillates between “pair insertions/deletions” (to maintain |A|=|B|) and single-point insertions (Section 4). It is unclear whether the structure supports arbitrary single updates while preserving a perfect matching or reverts to partial matching.

Baselines are limited to static recomputation variants; no comparison to popular approximate OT methods amenable to incremental updates (e.g., entropic Sinkhorn warm starts, quantization-based accelerations) is provided.
The experiments do not measure recourse (number of changed edges) or the overhead of explicit reporting, despite the importance for real-time applications.
The relation between p and the theoretical ε, and how this translates to observed approximation factors, is not quantified.

Several key technical claims are deferred to appendices (e.g., “only two points change per cell after an update,” the low-edit implicit matching update), but the main text depends on them critically; readers cannot fully assess soundness from the body.
Minor inconsistencies/typos and formatting artifacts (e.g., random symbols, duplicated citations) distract, and the dependence on dimension d beyond 2 is asserted but not articulated.
While dynamic optimal transport (Xu & Ding) is discussed, the paper could better position its geometric approach against incremental entropic OT or streaming/quantization EMD estimators.

The lower bound that (2−δ)-approximation with sublinear updates is impossible is only cited from an appendix (not included here); given its importance to contextualize the O(1/ε) factor, more visibility is warranted.
reviews.questions:
1. How do you reconcile the O(n^ε) per-update claim with Lemma 3.1’s O(p^5 log p log n) and the leaf Hungarian O(p^6) bounds when p^2 is stated to be Θ(n^ε)? What precise choice of p as a function of n and ε yields your final update bound, and how does this affect the “O(n^ε)-subcell” and “O(ε^{-1})-levels” statements?
2. Can you clarify the adversary model for the expected approximation guarantee? Does the bound hold against an adaptive adversary after a single random shift, or do you assume an oblivious adversary/update sequence?
3. Are updates restricted to paired insertions/deletions of one red and one blue point to maintain |A|=|B| at all times? If single-point updates are allowed (as in Section 4), what object is maintained (perfect vs. maximum-cardinality matching) and how does this affect time/approximation?
4. For the “improved dynamic algorithm” maintaining explicit matchings, could you summarize in the main text the key lemma that limits per-update changes to a short alternating path and provide its worst-case recourse bound?
5. Is there a faster specialized solver for the per-cell aggregated transportation instances that improves on O(p^5) by exploiting the grid or geometric structure (e.g., sparsity, Monge-like properties), thereby making the O(n^ε) bound straightforward?
6. In experiments, how do p and ε map numerically, and what is the empirical approximation factor vs. p (with error bars)? Could you also report memory usage and explicit recourse statistics?
7. Could you provide a brief outline of the (2−δ) lower bound construction in the main paper (assumptions, model, reduction target), given its centrality in motivating the approximation tradeoff?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles an important and timely problem and proposes a novel and practically effective dynamic approach to Euclidean bi-chromatic matching with demonstrable benefits for dynamic Wasserstein estimation. Conceptually, the bottom-up implicit-matching framework is elegant, and the empirical results are compelling. However, there is a significant gap in the time-complexity exposition: as written, the per-cell subroutines and leaf recomputations appear too expensive to support the stated O(n^ε) worst-case update time under the parameter choices described. This mismatch, along with an unspecified adversary model for the expected guarantee and ambiguity about the update model (paired vs. single insertions), prevents me from fully endorsing the theoretical claims. I believe the paper can reach the bar with a careful re-calibration of parameters (or faster subroutines), a clarified adversary/update model, and a more thorough main-text presentation of critical lemmas that underpin the dynamic updates. Given the strong potential impact and promising empirical validation, I encourage a revision that addresses these concerns.
